source.reference: SciBench dataset, source: Engel & Reid 'Thermodynamics, Statistical Thermodynamics, & Kinetics', problem 9.8. Verified by manual Raoult's law calculation: P_total = x_benzene * P_benzene_sat + x_hexane * P_hexane_sat = 0.28 * 120 + 0.72 * 189 = 33.6 + 136.08 = 169.68 ≈ 170 Torr.

problem.statement: At 303 K, the vapor pressure of pure benzene is 120 Torr and that of pure hexane is 189 Torr. Calculate the total vapor pressure of a solution in which the mole fraction of benzene is 0.28, assuming ideal solution behavior.
problem.task: Calculate: (1) partial pressure of benzene, (2) partial pressure of hexane, (3) total vapor pressure of the solution, (4) mole fraction of benzene in the vapor phase.

vapor_pressure_benzene.description: Vapor pressure of pure benzene at 303 K

vapor_pressure_hexane.description: Vapor pressure of pure hexane at 303 K

mole_fraction_benzene.description: Mole fraction of benzene in the liquid solution

mole_fraction_hexane.description: Mole fraction of hexane in the liquid solution

partial_pressure_benzene.description: Partial pressure of benzene = x_benzene * P_benzene_sat

partial_pressure_hexane.description: Partial pressure of hexane = x_hexane * P_hexane_sat

vapor_mole_fraction_benzene.description: Mole fraction of benzene in vapor = P_benzene / P_total

total_vapor_pressure._comment: Sum of two partials, slightly wider tolerance

must_include: Apply Raoult's law to calculate partial pressures
Sum partial pressures for total vapor pressure
State or use the ideal solution assumption
must_not_include: Use activity coefficients for an ideal solution
Confuse liquid mole fraction with vapor mole fraction

domain_context.recommended_thermo_model: Raoult's law (appropriate for ideal solutions — benzene and hexane are both nonpolar hydrocarbons)
key_assumptions: Ideal solution behavior (both components are nonpolar hydrocarbons of similar size)
common_mistakes: Forgetting to calculate the hexane mole fraction as (1 - x_benzene)
Reporting only total pressure without showing partial pressures
Confusing liquid composition (x) with vapor composition (y)
Using the wrong mole fraction for each component's partial pressure

reasoning_checkpoints.checkpoint: Identifies this as an ideal solution problem where Raoult's law applies directly
Calculates both partial pressures using P_i = x_i * P_i_sat
Sums partial pressures to get total vapor pressure
Calculates vapor phase composition using y_i = P_i / P_total
States the ideal solution assumption and why it is appropriate
